top_findings.finding: Screen reader user cannot access video player controls, which are not exposed to NVDA, preventing full interaction with video content.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant attempted to access video player controls using NVDA screen reader and found they were not visible or announced to the assistive technology.
top_findings.stated_signal: The player controls don't seem to be accessible here to my screen reader, they're not showing at all. Screen media users like myself are being shut out here, which is a real shame.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated that player controls are not visible to NVDA and expressed frustration about being excluded. The observed inability to access controls via screen reader combined with the stated friction indicates a complete accessibility barrier for a specific component (video player controls), blocking that task while transcripts remain accessible. This matches S2 (component task blocked) and L4 (severe friction with near-abandonment due to exclusion).
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users (NVDA) experience structural exclusion from video player controls due to missing accessibility markup or semantic labeling. This is a cohort-specific blocker that sighted users would not encounter.

top_findings.finding: Screen reader users cannot determine link purpose when navigating by tab key alone because generic link text 'get a quote' lacks context about what the quote is for.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant identified that the link would only announce 'get a quote' to a screen reader user tabbing through the page, making it impossible to know the quote's purpose without reading surrounding text.
top_findings.stated_signal: If I was just tapping around, I wouldn't want to click that link because I wouldn't know what it is that I am getting a quote for. So that link should be renamed to get a quote for, and then what it is that the quote is there for.
top_findings.rationale: The participant, a screen reader user, identified a significant accessibility barrier: generic link text that violates WCAG guidelines for link naming. The link cannot be understood in isolation by users relying on keyboard or AT navigation. The stated concern and observed identification of the failure both point to significant friction that would cause screen reader users to skip or misactivate the link.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for screen reader and keyboard-only users. Sighted mouse users can infer link purpose from surrounding visual context, but AT users cannot access that context when tabbing through isolated link announcements.

top_findings.finding: Videos on the page do not play when accessed with a screen reader, preventing the participant from accessing video content.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant attempted to access videos on the page with the screen reader and found they would not play.
top_findings.stated_signal: These videos with the screen reader don't seem to want to play, which is interesting.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated that videos are not accessible with the screen reader. This is a direct accessibility friction event—the video functionality does not work correctly with assistive technology. The participant's tone shows mild frustration ('which is interesting'), and this represents a significant barrier to content access that was worked around or skipped.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users are structurally blocked from video playback when videos lack proper ARIA labels, keyboard controls, or alternative accessible implementations. This cohort cannot access the content via the standard video player.

top_findings.finding: Video player controls are not accessible to screen reader users, preventing blind and low-vision users from operating the videos independently.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant identified that player controls are not visible to NVDA and described being shut out from video functionality.
top_findings.stated_signal: The player controls are not visible to NVDA, so that needs to be fixed because screen media users like myself are being shut out here.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated that accessibility controls for screen readers are missing on video players, which is a structural accessibility barrier. While transcripts exist as a workaround, the participant expressed that it is not equivalent to watching the videos. This represents significant friction for screen reader users accessing multimedia content, met by the S3 definition (significant component failure or barrier worked around).
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users (blind and low-vision) experience structurally amplified friction: they cannot independently access video player controls at all, forcing reliance on transcripts as an incomplete workaround, whereas sighted users can see and operate controls without impediment.

top_findings.finding: Participant using screen reader could not access embedded videos on the page, resulting in inability to hear Paralympian speakers and missing significant content.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant navigated the page with screen reader but could not play or hear the videos; explicitly stated the videos were not accessible.
top_findings.stated_signal: If those videos can be sorted so they play directly on the page with the screen reader, then this would be great because I feel like I've not got the full effect of the page here because I've not been able to hear the Paralympians speak, I've not been able to watch those videos, so I feel like I've missed out on this.
top_findings.rationale: The participant's screen reader did not provide accessible playback of embedded video content, preventing access to a core component of the page. Both observed behaviour (inability to interact with videos) and stated signal (explicit description of missing content and disappointment) align on a significant accessibility barrier that degraded the user's experience of the page.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users are structurally blocked from video content when videos lack proper accessible player controls, captions, or transcripts. This is a documented accessibility requirement gap for blind/low-vision users.

coaching_recommendations.title: Prioritise task-blocking friction before general coaching
coaching_recommendations.summary: The session contains 25 finding-level issue(s), including task-blocking or near-blocking severity (S2).
advice: Review the highest-severity findings before applying general coaching templates.
Prioritise issues that prevented or substantially disrupted task completion.
Use the highest-severity findings to guide focused follow-up coaching.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A severity distribution: S2=1, S3=12, S4=5, S5=6, S6=1. Representative findings: Screen reader user cannot access video player controls, which are not exposed to NVDA, preventing full interaction with video content. | Screen reader users cannot determine link purpose when navigating by tab key alone because generic link text 'get a quote' lacks context about what the quote is for. | Videos on the page do not play when accessed with a screen reader, preventing the participant from accessing video content.

coaching_recommendations.title: Multi-pattern friction across 5 categories — anchor coaching on the dominant pattern
coaching_recommendations.summary: This session shows 25 findings spanning 5 distinct friction types (F3,F1,F2,F4,F7). Coaching should follow the dominant pattern first rather than issuing one item per type.
advice: After addressing any severity-priority recommendation first, use this aggregation to group the remaining coaching themes — do not treat the two as parallel.
Treat F3 (Accessibility) as the primary coaching anchor; secondary types (F1 Comprehension, F2 Confidence) provide supporting context.
Avoid issuing parallel recommendations for each friction type when patterns interact within the same session.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A: total=25 findings across 5 distinct friction types (F3=17, F1=4, F2=2, F4=1, F7=1).

coaching_recommendations.title: Recording quality is usable but should be improved
coaching_recommendations.summary: The session is still usable for analysis, but defects in the recording may reduce confidence in detailed interpretation.
advice: Check microphone clarity and ambient noise before starting future sessions.
Reduce clipping, brief audio gaps, or interruptions where possible.
Mark this session as usable with caution if fine-grained analysis is required.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-B marks recording quality as acceptable rather than good.
